Off Calendar Feb-11-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
W. Joe Gunter of CMBG3 LAW LLC (415-957-2325), appearing for defendant and opposing party Keenan Properties, Inc.

RULING: PLAINTIFFS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUCATION AGAINST DEFENDANT KEENAN PROPERTIES, INC.
No other appearances and tentative ruling adopted: Plaintiffs' motion for summary adjudication is denied. Plaintiffs failed to sustain their burden of demonstrating that Defendant Keenan Properties, Inc. does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence as to each one of its affirmative defenses. (See Decl. Mirzayn, Ex. A at p. 16, Ex. B. at p. 153-159, Ex. C at p. 18, Ex. D at p. 21, Ex. E at p. 190-96, Ex. F at 24-25.) The burden of production "should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Construction Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 83.) Objections raised during discovery "are not the equivalent of a factually devoid responses, and therefore cannot be used to shift the burden . . .." (Gaggero v. Yura (2003) 108 Cal.App.4th 884, 893.) Even if Defendant Keenan Properties, Inc.'s discovery responses are factually devoid, Plaintiffs did not conduct comprehensive discovery. A party cannot rely on factually devoid discovery responses in the absence of conducting comprehensive discovery. (Scheiding, supra, 69 Cal.App.4th at 83; Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 854-855.) The Court "can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered." Scheiding, supra, 69 Cal.App.4th at 81.) The Court notes that Plaintiffs failed to comply with California Rules of Court, rule 3.1350(h) in preparing their separate statement of undisputed material facts. Moreover, pursuant to SF Local Rule 2.7B, the Court did not receive courtesy copies of Plaintiffs' reply papers and thus Plaintiffs' reply was not considered. Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: A. Mok. Not reported. (503/CML)

Summary judgment must "be granted if all the papers submitted show that there is no triable issue as to any material fact and that the moving party is entitled to judgment as a matter of law." (CCP 437c(c).) Defendant ConocoPhillips' motion for summary judgment is denied because Plaintiffs' causes of action for premise liability and loss of consortium survive summary adjudication.

Defendant ConocoPhillips' motion for summary adjudication as to Plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages is denied. Defendant ConocoPhillips failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain clear and convincing evidence that Defendant ConocoPhillips acted with the requisite malice, fraud, or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. (See Decl. Savage, Ex. A-1 at p. 9, Ex. A-2 at p. 7, Ex. A-3 at p.4, Ex. B-1 at 34-41, Ex. B-2 at p. 22-24, Ex. B at p. 5.) The burden of production "should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Construction Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 83.) Objections raised during discovery "are not the equivalent of a factually devoid responses, and therefore cannot be used to shift the burden from the defendant to the plaintiff under Code of Civil Procedure section 437c, subdivision (p)(2)." (Gaggero v. Yura (2003) 108 Cal.App.4th 884, 893.) Even if Plaintiffs' discovery responses are factually devoid, Defendant ConocoPhillips did not conduct comprehensive discovery as to punitive damages. Defendant cannot rely on Plaintiffs' factually devoid discovery responses in the absence of conducting comprehensive discovery. (Scheiding, supra, 69 Cal.App.4th at 83; Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 854-855.) The Court "can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered." MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Feb-07-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Sara J. Savage of HUGO & PARKER, LLP (415-808-0300), appearing for defendant and moving party ConocoPhillips Company.
Stephen M. Fishback of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiffs and opposing parties Danny Black and Debra Black.

RULING: Defendant ConocoPhillips' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AND ADJUCATION
Hearing held, argued and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant ConocoPhillips Co.'s motion for summary judgment, or in the alternative, summary adjudication is denied.

Considering all evidence and inferences in favor of Plaintiffs, Defendant ConocoPhillips failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence regarding Plaintiffs' cause of action for premises liability, Plaintiffs' cause of action for loss of consortium, and Plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages. (CCP 437c(p)(2); Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 843, 850-51.) The burden of production "should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Construction Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 83.) Defendant cannot rely on Plaintiffs' factually devoid discovery responses in the absence of conducting comprehensive discovery. (Id. at 83; Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at 854-855.)

Defendant's motion for summary adjudication as to Plaintiffs' cause of action for premises liability is denied. "[W]hen the landowner knows or should know of a concealed hazard on its premises, then under ordinary premises liability principles, the landowner may be liable for a resultant injury to those employees." (Kinsmann v. Unocal Corp. (2005) 37 Cal.4th 659, 674.) Mr. Black testified that he worked at the Tosco refinery near Martinez, CA for six to eight week, working 8-16 hour shifts, 5 days a week. (Decl. Savage, Ex. C, Depo. Black at 990:8-15, 990:22-991:23, 991:25-992:1.) Mr. Black was an employee of Oscar Erickson and not Tesco Co. (Id. at 996:10-997:3.) Mr. Black stated that while working at the Tosco refinery he performed clean up work, he worked around Tosco employees scraping gaskets, he removed gaskets himself, and that he removed asbestos thermal insulation. (Id. at 1002:1-1005:8-17.) Defendant ConocoPhillips failed to shift burden because (1) ConocoPhillips failed to show that it did not know or reasonably should have known of the pre-existing hazardous condition at the Tosco refinery; (2) ConocoPhilips failed to demonstrate that Oscar Erickson knew or could reasonably ascertain the condition; and (3) ConocoPhillips failed to present any evidence that it warned Oscar Erickson of the hazard. (See Kinsmann v. Unocal Corp., supra, 37 Cal.4th at 675.) Even if Defendant ConocoPhillips shifted burden, the declarations of Mr. Black and Charles Ay create triable issues of material fact as to whether Mr. Black was exposed to asbestos while working at the Tosco refinery. (See Decl. Mirzayan, Ex. B, Ex. C.)
(Mini Minutes continued, see next entry)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION - Parker-Hannifin Corporations Notice Of Motions And Motions For Summary Adjudication Of The False Representation And Intentional Tort Causes Of Action And Claim For Punitive Damages
Off Calendar Feb-06-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
Stephen M. Fishback of KELLER, FISHBACK & JACKSON LLP (818-342-7442), appearing for plaintiff Danny Black.
Joseph B. Adams of BASSI EDLIN HUIE & BLUM (415-397-9006), appearing for defendant Parker-Hannifin Corporation.

RULING: DEFENDANT PARKER-HANNIFIN CORPORATION MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION OF THE FALSE REPRESENTATION AND INTENTIONAL TORT CAUSES OF ACTION AND CLAIM FOR PUNITIVE DAMAGES
Court denies moving party's late notice to contest and the tentative ruling is adopted: Defendant Parker-Hannifin Corp.'s motion for summary adjudication is denied in its entirety. Considering all evidence and inferences in favor of Plaintiffs, Defendant Parker-Hannifin Corp. failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence regarding Plaintiffs' third cause of action, fourth causes of action, and claim for punitive damages. (CCP 437c(p)(2); Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 843, 850-51.) The burden of production "should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Construction Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 83.) Defendant cannot rely on Plaintiffs' factually devoid discovery responses in the absence of conducting comprehensive discovery. (Id. at 83; Aguilar, supra, 25 Cal.4th at 854-855.) Defendant Parker-Hannifin Corp.'s motion for summary adjudication is denied as to Plaintiffs' third cause of action for false representation and fourth cause of action for intentional tort under Civil Code sections 1708, 1709, 1710. Even if Plaintiffs' discovery responses were factually devoid, Defendant Parker-Hannifin Corp. failed to conduct comprehensive discovery as to either Plaintiffs' third cause of action or fourth cause of action. Nowhere in Defendant Parker-Hannifin Corp.'s special interrogatories to plaintiffs, set one does Defendant Parker-Hannifin Corp. ask about Plaintiffs' false representation claim or the intentional tort claim. (See Decl. Bassi, Ex. B, Ex. C., Ex. D., Ex. E.) Moreover, Defendant Parker Hannifin Corp. failed to address Plaintiffs' third and fourth causes of action in their request for production of documents. (See Decl. Bassi, Ex. F, Ex. G.) Nor did Defendant Parker Hannifin Corp. ask questions of Mr. Black as to his claim of false representation or his claims under Civil Code sections 1708, 1709, 1710. (See Decl. Bassi, Ex. H.) The Court "can infer nothing at all with respect to questions which were neither asked nor answered." (See Scheiding, supra, 69 Cal.App.4th at 81.) Defendant Parker-Hannifin Corp.'s motion for summary adjudication as to Plaintiffs' claims for punitive damages is denied. A claim for punitive damages under Civil Code section 3294 requires a showing that Parker-Hannifin Corp. carried on despicable conduct with an awareness of the probable dangerous consequences, and willfully failed to avoid such consequences. (See Pfeifer v. John Crane (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1270, 1299-1301[citing Angie M. v. Superior Court (1995) 37 Cal.App.4th 1217, 1228].) Mr. Black claims he was exposed to Defendant Parker Hannifin Corp. and Sacomo-Sierra's asbestos-containing products from the 1960s to the 1980s. (Decl. Bassi, Ex. C at p. 5.)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT - Alta Building Material Co.S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Jan-29-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT ALTA BUILDING MATERIAL CO'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

Appearances: Edward E. Hartley of Hassard Bonnington LLP (415.288.9800) for defendant and moving party; Bruce Jackson of Keller Fishback & Jackson (818.342.7442) for plaintiff and responding party

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Alta Building Material Co.'s motion for summary judgment and alternative motion for summary adjudication are denied.

Defendant Alta Building Material Co.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Taking all evidence and inferences in favor of Plaintiffs, Defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiffs do not possess, and cannot reasonable obtain evidence that Mr. Black was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials sold, supplied or distributed by Defendant Alta Building Material Co. and therefore there exists no evidence that Defendant caused Mr. Black's alleged injuries. Mr. Black testified that he cut and stapled CertainTeed asbestos-containing roof felt in the early 1960s and identified Defendant Alta Building Material Co.'s as the supplier of the felt. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit A, Deposition Black at pp. 59:8-20, 66:5-22, 67:3-8, 438:17-22, 448:14-18, 1655:20-1656:14.) CertainTeed offered "Perforated Saturated Asbestos Felt" from the 1950s until 1975, which was serviced by its roofing plant in Richmond, California and offered for sale in the geographic region. (Decl. Edward E. Hartley, Exhibit R.) Defendant Alta Building Material Co. failed to shift burden under the factually devoid prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826.
Even if Defendant Alta Building Material Co. sustained its initial burden, Mr. Black's deposition testimony, the Declaration of Mr. Black, the Declaration of Charles Ay, and the Deposition of Sam Siri create a triable issues of fact as to whether Mr. Black was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials sold, supplied or distributed by Defendant Alta Building Material Co. (Decl. of Danny Black; Decl. Charles Ay; see Plaintiffs' Exhibit A, Deposition of Sam Siri.)

Defendant Alta Building Material Co.'s motion for summary adjudication of Plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages is denied. Defendant Alta Building Material Co. failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain clear and convincing evidence that Defendant Alta Building Material Co. acted with the requisite malice, fraud, or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. (See Decl. Edward E. Hartley, Exhibit C, Exhibit D, Exhibit E, Exhibit F.) The burden of production "should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Construction Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 83.) Even if Plaintiffs' discovery response were factually devoid, Defendant Alta Building Material Co. did not conduct comprehensive discovery as to punitive damages. Defendants cannot rely on a plaintiff's factually devoid discovery responses in the absence of conducting comprehensive discovery. (Id. at 83; see Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 102-103.) Order signed. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Angela Pourtabib, CSR 13714 (Tel: 415.601.7054 / Email: apourtabib@gmail.com)

MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT SLAKEY BROTHERS, INC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Appearances: Anthony Bentivegna of Bennett Samuelson Reynolds (415.444.6453) for defendant and moving party; Bruce Jackson of Keller Fishback & Jackson (818.342.7442) for plaintiff and responding party.

Argued and the Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Slakey Brothers, Inc.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. Viewing all inferences in favor of Plaintiffs, Defendant Slakey Brothers Inc. failed to shift burden. (CCP 437c(p)(2); Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 843, 850-51.) Mr. Black testified that he worked at the Rainbow Bakery facility located in Sacramento from 1986 to 2006. (Plaintiffs' Exhibit A, Deposition of Danny Black (Depo. Black) at p. 826:12-21; see Defendant's Exhibit 11.) Mr. Black's job duties included replacing gaskets and packing on pumps and valves. (Depo. Black at pp. 831:3-4, 831:14-832:1, 832:7-13, 823:14-20.) Mr. Black testified that the gaskets and packing were acquired or ordered from Slakey Brothers Inc. and recalls seeing Slakey Brothers Inc. trucks unload deliveries. (Depo. Black at pp. 1116:13-16, 1127:9-12.)

Even if Slakey Brothers Inc. shifted burden, Plaintiffs provide evidence that raise triable issues of fact. (CCP 437c(p)(2); Saelzer v. Advanced Group (2001) 25 Cal.4th 763, 780-781.) Slakey Brothers Inc. invoices demonstrate that Slakey Brothers Inc. in Sacramento delivered products to the Rainbow Bakery facility. (See Plaintiffs' Exhibit B.) The invoices, Mr. Black's deposition testimony, the Declaration of Mr. Black, and the Declaration of Charles Ay raise triable issues of fact as to Mr. Black's exposure to asbestos-containing products supplied by Defendant Slakey Brothers Inc. (See Plaintiffs' Exhibit C, Decl. of Danny Black; see Decl. Charles Ay at 20-21.) Order signed. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: E. Off Calendar Jan-29-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT VALLEY PIPE & SUPPLY, INC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION.

Appearances: Bruce Jackson of Keller Fishback & Jackson (818.342.7442) for plaintiff and responding party; No other appearances.

This matter is not argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Valley Pipe & Supply, Inc.'s motion for summary adjudication of Plaintiffs' claim for punitive damages is denied. Defendant Valley Pipe & Supply, Inc. failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that Plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain clear and convincing evidence that Defendant Valley Pipe & Supply, Inc. acted with the requisite malice, fraud, or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. (See Decl. Scott R. Herndon, Exhibit E, Exhibit F, Exhibit G, Exhibit H.) The burden of production "should not shift without stringent review of the direct, circumstantial and inferential evidence." (Scheiding v. Dinwiddie Construction Co. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 83.) Even if Plaintiffs' discovery response were factually devoid, Defendant Valley Pipe & Supply Inc. did not conduct comprehensive discovery as to punitive damages. Defendants cannot rely on a plaintiff's factually devoid discovery responses in the absence of conducting comprehensive discovery. (Id. at 83; see Andrews v. Foster Wheeler LLC (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 96, 102-103.) Order signed. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, Clerk: E. RULING:
PLAINTIFFS' DEMURRER TO DEFENDANT UNION CARBIDE CORP.'S ANSWER TO PLAINTIFFS' COMPLAINT

Hearing held. Court did not allow plaintiff to argue due to the late notice of intention to argue. The Court adopted its tentative ruling: Plaintiffs' demurrer to defendant Union Carbide Corp.'s answer to plaintiffs' complaint is overruled for the following reasons: Plaintiffs' demurrer violates CRC 3.1320(a) because it does not state each ground of demurrer in a separate paragraph. The Court will not consider plaintiffs' argument based on C.C.P. 431.30(g) as they did not identify that as a ground for demurrer in their Notice of Demurrer and Demurrer. Plaintiffs failed to demonstrate how the forty-five affirmative defenses to which they demur do not state facts sufficient to constitute a defense or are uncertain. (C.C.P. 430.20(a), (b).) Plaintiffs failed to meet and confer prior to filing their demurrer. (C.C.P. 430.41.) Counsel to submit a proposed order in compliance with C.R.C. 3.1312.
